problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem C (data type), team 2101166, Outstanding (O) award. The WSDA Asian giant hornet problem: given ~4,400 citizen sightings (timestamps, lat/long, photos, and a 4-way status label of positive/negative/unverified/unprocessed), the team had to (1) predict short-term spread of the hornet and assess prediction accuracy, (2) build a classifier that decides whether a photo contains a hornet and characterize what drives "negative" outputs, (3) prioritize which unverified/unprocessed sightings the government should investigate, (4) decide when/how to update the model as more data arrive, and (5) define indicators showing when the pest can be declared eradicated. The dataset is severely imbalanced (only ~14-19 true positives vs ~2,000 negatives after cleaning), which is the central data-handling challenge the whole paper is built around.
models_used: Problem 1 spread: a yearly time-step (discrete) difference equation simulated by Monte Carlo. Each nest i produces Q_i new fertilized queens drawn from a Poisson distribution Q_i ~ Poisson(r*E_i/(1+gamma*C_i)); each queen disperses to cell j with probability P_ij = exp(-Delta_ij/mu)*T_j. Two engineered metrics drive it: resource competition coefficient C_i (sum of a Gaussian kernel exp(-Delta_ij^2/2*sigma_c^2) over nests) and environmental friendliness E_i (same Gaussian kernel times terrain mask T_j, with T_j=0 on water, 1 on land). Initial state = 3 nests from clustering positives.

Logistic growth model f(t)=N*e^{r(t-t0)}/(1+N(e^{r(t-t0)}-1)) fitted to the simulated nest-count trajectory as an INDEPENDENT accuracy check (RMSProp optimizer, MSE loss = 0.076; fastest-growth year t0=7, i.e. 2027).

Problem 2 feature extraction: a convolutional Auto-Encoder (4 conv blocks encoder: Conv3x3+ReLU+BatchNorm+MaxPool, channels 3->6->12->24->48; symmetric deconv decoder with ConvTranspose2d). Trained with MSE reconstruction loss; min test loss 0.0274. The encoder bottleneck is reused as a feature compressor for all downstream classifiers.

Problem 2 classification: three competing models on the auto-encoder features — Binary Logistic Regression (L2 cost, Newton's method, acc 0.5303), SVM with Gaussian/RBF kernel (gamma swept 0..2 step 0.01, best gamma=1.64, acc 0.5758), and a CNN (2 conv blocks + BatchNorm + 2 FC layers, 25% dropout, Cross-Entropy loss, Softmax; acc 0.8030 at 490 iters). CNN selected.

Problem 2 data augmentation: image flipping (vertical/horizontal/diagonal) plus Borderline-SMOTE oversampling of the minority positive class (48 -> 697 synthetic samples to reach >=1:4 positive:negative ratio).

Problem 2 statistical screening: a simple linear regression / one-way ANOVA of comment-length on status (y = beta0 + beta1*x + eps; F-significance 0.072 > 0.05) used to JUSTIFY dropping the text comments and using images only.

Problem 3 region division: Agglomerative (hierarchical) Clustering on sighting lat/long using single-linkage; cluster count chosen via Silhouette coefficient sweep (k=2..9) plus a domain constraint (max intra-cluster diameter must fit one government inspection; 329km at k=2 vs 190km at k=5 => k=5).

Problem 3 priority: region priority = probability that at least one image in the cluster is positive, Pr(x)=1 - prod(1 - Pr(x_i)), with Pr(x_i) the CNN positive-probability; ranks the 5 clusters (Seattle-area cluster 0 highest at 0.9982).

Problem 4 update cadence: cubic-spline interpolation of metric-vs-time-threshold curves (auto-encoder test loss, CNN test accuracy), then central-difference numerical derivative; the threshold at the derivative extremum defines the optimal retraining interval (auto-encoder ~4 months, CNN ~3 months).

Problem 5 eradication metric: a composite Evaluation Coefficient K_t = g_t * d_t * p_t (growth ratio of positives, distribution-range ratio of pairwise nest distances, positive-observation ratio), corrected for classifier error rate theta via a Bayesian/posterior update; K -> 0 signals eradication. Validated on a simulated grow-then-cull logistic trajectory.
key_techniques: Feature engineering driven by biology: encoding 'competition' and 'habitat quality' as Gaussian spatial kernels with a binary land/water terrain mask, instead of using a generic SIR/diffusion PDE — makes the spread model interpretable and ecology-grounded.
Stochastic (Monte Carlo) simulation with Poisson-distributed reproduction rather than deterministic iteration, explicitly to inject realistic noise and produce a distribution of outcomes.

Cross-validating one model with a second independent model: using a closed-form logistic curve fit as a quantitative sanity check on the simulation output, turning 'is my model right?' into a reported loss number (0.076).

Two-stage pipeline (auto-encoder feature compression -> lightweight classifier) so that the tiny positive sample set is classified in a low-dimensional space; this is the move that let a CNN reach 0.80 on a dataset with ~19 positives.
Aggressive, layered class-imbalance handling: flips for cheap augmentation + Borderline-SMOTE for synthetic minority samples, with an explicit target ratio (1:4) cited from literature.
Using statistics to PRUNE the problem: an ANOVA proves comments are uninformative, which both simplifies the pipeline and shows judges rigorous decision-making.
Turning model selection into a defensible objective: silhouette score AND a real-world operational constraint (inspection radius) jointly pick k=5 clusters.
Reframing 'when to retrain' as a calculus problem: spline + central-difference derivative extremum gives a crisp, defensible number instead of a hand-wave.
Composing a single scalar decision index (K_t) from multiple ratio indicators and Bayes-correcting it for the known classifier error — gives the WSDA one number to watch.
why_it_won: "It answers all five sub-questions with a DIFFERENT, appropriately-matched method each, yet ties them into one coherent pipeline (clustering seeds the spread model; the CNN feeds both prioritization and the eradication index). Judges reward this end-to-end integration. It directly confronts the defining difficulty of this data problem — extreme class imbalance with only ~19 positives — and shows a layered, literature-cited remedy rather than ignoring it. Almost every modeling choice is justified with evidence: a logistic cross-check loss for the simulation, an ANOVA to drop comments, a silhouette+operational-radius argument for cluster count, a derivative-extremum for update cadence. Quantitative results are reported honestly and concretely (0.0274 AE loss, 0.8030 CNN accuracy, per-cluster priorities, Seattle as top risk). It blends domain biology (annual life cycle motivating a yearly time step, queen fertilization rate 0.35, 49.5 km/yr spread from European studies) with modern ML, signaling both modeling maturity and reading of the literature. Finally it delivers the required WSDA memorandum with actionable, specific operational guidance (retrain intervals, priority-ordered handling, public-education tips about photo backgrounds)."
section_highlights: "Summary page front-loads every model name and headline number (losses, accuracies, the 4-month/3-month cadence, Seattle), so a judge sees the whole solution in one screen. Each problem section opens with a Method Overview + a workflow flowchart, then Model Construction, then Results — consistent, skimmable structure. Notation is centralized in a parameter table (Table 1) before the math. Equations are numbered and each parameter is verbally interpreted in ecological terms right after the formula. Algorithm 1 gives clean pseudocode for the Monte Carlo spread simulation. Results are shown as maps over years (2020/2023/2029), loss/accuracy curves, dendrograms, and a 3D sensitivity surface. A dedicated Strengths/Weaknesses + Sensitivity Analysis section (varying r, gamma, and negative-image count) shows self-awareness. Core PyTorch code for the auto-encoder and CNN is included in appendices, making the deep-learning claims reproducible. The closing WSDA memorandum translates math into plain operational advice."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The CNN's 0.80 'accuracy' is measured on a test set built largely from Borderline-SMOTE-augmented/flipped positives derived from only ~19 real positives, with a hand-balanced test split — so the headline number almost certainly overstates real-world performance on genuine novel photos; no precision/recall/AUC or confusion matrix is reported despite the imbalance.
Assumption that WSDA's status labels are error-free is used to train the classifier, yet Problem 5 simultaneously assumes the classifier has error rate theta — a mild internal tension.

Spread-model hyperparameters (r, gamma, mu, sigma_c) are essentially fit/assumed from very sparse data (3 initial nests); the team admits this. Treating fitted hyperparameters as spatially constant across all of Washington is a strong, unverified assumption.

Auto-encoder reconstruction loss measures image compression fidelity, not class-discriminative feature quality — a low 0.0274 AE loss does not guarantee good features for hornet detection, though the paper implicitly leans on it.

The Problem 5 Bayesian correction formula (Eq. 19) is loosely specified/notationally muddled (the same term appears in numerator and denominator), and K_t multiplies three ratios without justifying independence; it reads more as a heuristic index than a rigorous posterior.
Cluster priority via Pr(at-least-one-positive) is dominated by cluster size (more images -> higher union probability), which can conflate 'many reports' with 'high hornet likelihood'.
Several rendered figures/data tables in this markdown are OCR-garbled (e.g., the donut 'life cycle' and some maps), so some quantitative figure values are unreliable as transcribed.
reusable_patterns: Two-stage 'auto-encoder compress -> simple classifier' pipeline is a reusable template for any MCM data problem with high-dimensional inputs (images, signals) and few labeled samples.

Engineer interpretable spatial features as Gaussian distance kernels times a domain mask (here terrain land/water) when a problem mixes geography + competition + suitability — generalizes to disease spread, facility siting, wildlife range.
Validate a stochastic simulation by fitting an independent closed-form curve (logistic) to its output and reporting the fit loss as an 'accuracy' metric.
Justify dropping a data modality with a quick significance test (ANOVA/regression) — both simplifies and demonstrates rigor.
Choose a hyperparameter (number of clusters, etc.) by combining an intrinsic metric (silhouette) with a real-world operational constraint, and show the trade (k=2 vs k=5 diameters).
Convert a 'how often / when' question into the extremum of a numerical derivative of a metric-vs-parameter curve (spline interpolation + central difference) for a crisp defensible answer.

Build one scalar decision index from several normalized ratio indicators (each a year-over-year ratio) and Bayes-correct it for known measurement error, then validate the index on a synthetic rise-and-fall scenario.
Layered class-imbalance recipe: cheap geometric augmentation (flips) + Borderline-SMOTE to a literature-cited target ratio, plus saving the best-on-test checkpoint to fight overfitting.

Report-craft pattern: model-name-and-number-dense summary, per-problem (overview flowchart -> construction -> results) structure, centralized notation table, sensitivity section, and a stakeholder memorandum that restates results as operational actions.
